turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Good morning, Mr. Johnson. What brings you in today?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: I've been having this pressure in my chest. It started about two hours ago.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Can you describe what it feels like?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: Like something heavy sitting on my chest. Dull, not sharp.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Does it radiate anywhere — your arm, jaw, back?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: A little into my left arm, and up to my jaw.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: On a scale of one to ten, how bad is it right now?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: Around a six. It was worse when I was walking to the car.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Anything make it better or worse?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: It got better when I sat down. Worse with walking.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Any shortness of breath, sweating, or nausea?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: I felt sweaty at first. A little short of breath too.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: You said it started two hours ago — was this the first episode, or have you felt it before?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: Actually, now that I think about it, I had something similar yesterday evening after dinner. It went away in a few minutes so I didn't mention it.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Got it. Any history of heart disease, high blood pressure, or diabetes?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: High blood pressure for about ten years. My father had a heart attack at fifty-eight.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: What medications are you on?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: Amlodipine for my blood pressure, atorvastatin for cholesterol, and a daily aspirin.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Any smoking or alcohol?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: I smoked a pack a day for thirty years, quit five years ago.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: I want to get an ECG and a troponin, and apply the HEART score to assess your risk.

full_text_reference: Good morning, Mr. Johnson. What brings you in today? I've been having this pressure in my chest. It started about two hours ago. Can you describe what it feels like? Like something heavy sitting on my chest. Dull, not sharp. Does it radiate anywhere — your arm, jaw, back? A little into my left arm, and up to my jaw. On a scale of one to ten, how bad is it right now? Around a six. It was worse when I was walking to the car. Anything make it better or worse? It got better when I sat down. Worse with walking. Any shortness of breath, sweating, or nausea? I felt sweaty at first. A little short of breath too. You said it started two hours ago — was this the first episode, or have you felt it before? Actually, now that I think about it, I had something similar yesterday evening after dinner. It went away in a few minutes so I didn't mention it. Got it. Any history of heart disease, high blood pressure, or diabetes? High blood pressure for about ten years. My father had a heart attack at fifty-eight. What medications are you on? Amlodipine for my blood pressure, atorvastatin for cholesterol, and a daily aspirin. Any smoking or alcohol? I smoked a pack a day for thirty years, quit five years ago. I want to get an ECG and a troponin, and apply the HEART score to assess your risk.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Good afternoon, Ms. Smith. Tell me what brought you in today.

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: I've had this pain in my belly since last night. It's getting worse.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Where exactly is the pain? Can you point to it?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: It started around my belly button, but now it's more on the lower right side.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: On a scale of one to ten, how bad is it right now?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: Maybe a seven. It's sharp, and it hurts more when I move or cough.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Any nausea, vomiting, or fever?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: I threw up once this morning. I feel warm but I didn't check my temperature.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: You said it started last night — what time exactly?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: Actually, thinking back, it was more like yesterday afternoon, around three. Not last night.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Understood. Any change in appetite or bowel movements?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: I haven't wanted to eat anything. No diarrhea.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Any medications you take regularly?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: Just ibuprofen for headaches, and a multivitamin. Nothing prescription.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Any prior surgeries?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: No, never.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: I'm going to examine your abdomen now. I'll press gently; tell me where it hurts most.

full_text_reference: Good afternoon, Ms. Smith. Tell me what brought you in today. I've had this pain in my belly since last night. It's getting worse. Where exactly is the pain? Can you point to it? It started around my belly button, but now it's more on the lower right side. On a scale of one to ten, how bad is it right now? Maybe a seven. It's sharp, and it hurts more when I move or cough. Any nausea, vomiting, or fever? I threw up once this morning. I feel warm but I didn't check my temperature. You said it started last night — what time exactly? Actually, thinking back, it was more like yesterday afternoon, around three. Not last night. Understood. Any change in appetite or bowel movements? I haven't wanted to eat anything. No diarrhea. Any medications you take regularly? Just ibuprofen for headaches, and a multivitamin. Nothing prescription. Any prior surgeries? No, never. I'm going to examine your abdomen now. I'll press gently; tell me where it hurts most.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Hello, Mr. Smith. What's going on today?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: I've been short of breath. It came on pretty suddenly.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: When did it start?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: This morning, maybe around eight. I was just sitting at my desk.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Any chest pain with it?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: A little. It's sharp, and it's worse when I take a deep breath.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Any cough, fever, or leg swelling?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: No cough, no fever. But my right calf has been sore for a few days.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Have you traveled recently or been immobile for long periods?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: I flew back from Europe last week. It was a long flight, maybe nine hours.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: You said it came on this morning — was it really sudden, or did it build up?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: Actually, now that I think about it, I noticed it a bit yesterday evening too. It just got much worse this morning.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Got it. Any history of asthma, heart problems, or clots?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: No asthma. My father had a DVT, I think.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Medications?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: I take lisinopril for blood pressure, and I'm on a daily aspirin.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Any smoking history?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: I quit about ten years ago. Smoked for twenty years before that.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: I want to check your oxygen saturation and listen to your lungs.

full_text_reference: Hello, Mr. Smith. What's going on today? I've been short of breath. It came on pretty suddenly. When did it start? This morning, maybe around eight. I was just sitting at my desk. Any chest pain with it? A little. It's sharp, and it's worse when I take a deep breath. Any cough, fever, or leg swelling? No cough, no fever. But my right calf has been sore for a few days. Have you traveled recently or been immobile for long periods? I flew back from Europe last week. It was a long flight, maybe nine hours. You said it came on this morning — was it really sudden, or did it build up? Actually, now that I think about it, I noticed it a bit yesterday evening too. It just got much worse this morning. Got it. Any history of asthma, heart problems, or clots? No asthma. My father had a DVT, I think. Medications? I take lisinopril for blood pressure, and I'm on a daily aspirin. Any smoking history? I quit about ten years ago. Smoked for twenty years before that. I want to check your oxygen saturation and listen to your lungs.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Hi, Ms. Smith. What brings you in today?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: I've got a really bad headache. I've never had one like this.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: When did it start?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: About two hours ago. It hit me all at once, like something exploded in my head.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Where is the pain?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: All over, but mostly the back of my head and my neck feels stiff.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: On a scale of one to ten?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: A ten. It's the worst headache of my life.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Any nausea, vomiting, or vision changes?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: I feel sick to my stomach, and bright light really bothers me.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Any weakness, numbness, or trouble speaking?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: No, nothing like that.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: You said this started two hours ago — was it sudden or gradual?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: Actually, it's been closer to an hour. I was trying to wait it out before coming in.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Okay. Any history of migraines?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: I get headaches sometimes, but they're never like this. This is different.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Any medications?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: I took two acetaminophen about thirty minutes ago. No relief. I'm also on sumatriptan for my usual migraines, but I haven't taken it today.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Any family history of aneurysm or stroke?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: My aunt had a brain bleed in her fifties.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: I'm going to examine your neck and do a neurologic exam now.

full_text_reference: Hi, Ms. Smith. What brings you in today? I've got a really bad headache. I've never had one like this. When did it start? About two hours ago. It hit me all at once, like something exploded in my head. Where is the pain? All over, but mostly the back of my head and my neck feels stiff. On a scale of one to ten? A ten. It's the worst headache of my life. Any nausea, vomiting, or vision changes? I feel sick to my stomach, and bright light really bothers me. Any weakness, numbness, or trouble speaking? No, nothing like that. You said this started two hours ago — was it sudden or gradual? Actually, it's been closer to an hour. I was trying to wait it out before coming in. Okay. Any history of migraines? I get headaches sometimes, but they're never like this. This is different. Any medications? I took two acetaminophen about thirty minutes ago. No relief. I'm also on sumatriptan for my usual migraines, but I haven't taken it today. Any family history of aneurysm or stroke? My aunt had a brain bleed in her fifties. I'm going to examine your neck and do a neurologic exam now.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Good morning, Mr. Smith. What brings you in?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: I've been really tired for months now. And I think I'm losing weight without trying.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: How much weight, and over what period?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: Maybe fifteen pounds over the last three months. My clothes are loose.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Any change in appetite?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: Actually, my appetite is okay — sometimes I feel hungrier than usual, but the weight keeps coming off.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Any night sweats or fevers?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: I wake up damp sometimes, yeah. Not every night.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: How's your mood been? Sleep?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: Sleep is okay. Mood, I guess I've been down, but I think it's because I feel so tired all the time.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: You said fifteen pounds — are you sure about that number?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: Let me think. It might be closer to twenty. I was 185, and I weighed myself this morning at 165.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Any bowel changes, blood in stool, or urinary changes?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: I go to the bathroom more than I used to. I'm thirsty all the time.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Any lumps, or a history of cancer in the family?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: No lumps I've noticed. My mother had breast cancer at sixty.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Any medications?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: I take metformin for pre-diabetes, and levothyroxine for my thyroid.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: I'd like to get some labs — a CBC, TSH, metabolic panel, and hemoglobin A1c.

full_text_reference: Good morning, Mr. Smith. What brings you in? I've been really tired for months now. And I think I'm losing weight without trying. How much weight, and over what period? Maybe fifteen pounds over the last three months. My clothes are loose. Any change in appetite? Actually, my appetite is okay — sometimes I feel hungrier than usual, but the weight keeps coming off. Any night sweats or fevers? I wake up damp sometimes, yeah. Not every night. How's your mood been? Sleep? Sleep is okay. Mood, I guess I've been down, but I think it's because I feel so tired all the time. You said fifteen pounds — are you sure about that number? Let me think. It might be closer to twenty. I was 185, and I weighed myself this morning at 165. Any bowel changes, blood in stool, or urinary changes? I go to the bathroom more than I used to. I'm thirsty all the time. Any lumps, or a history of cancer in the family? No lumps I've noticed. My mother had breast cancer at sixty. Any medications? I take metformin for pre-diabetes, and levothyroxine for my thyroid. I'd like to get some labs — a CBC, TSH, metabolic panel, and hemoglobin A1c.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Hello, Ms. Smith. Tell me what happened.

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: I passed out this morning. I was standing in the kitchen and the next thing I knew I was on the floor.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Any warning before you passed out?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: I felt lightheaded, a bit sweaty, and my vision went gray around the edges.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: How long were you out?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: My husband said maybe ten seconds. I came to quickly.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Any chest pain, palpitations, or shortness of breath before it happened?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: No chest pain. My heart felt like it was racing for a second, but it was quick.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Did you bite your tongue or lose bladder control?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: No, nothing like that.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: You said your heart was racing — was that before or after you felt lightheaded?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: Actually, thinking about it, the racing came first. Then the lightheaded feeling, then I went down.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Okay, that's important. Any similar episodes before?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: I've felt dizzy when I stand up quickly, but I've never passed out before.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Any recent illness, fluid loss, or changes in medication?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: I had a stomach bug two days ago, some diarrhea. I haven't been drinking much water.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: What medications do you take?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: I'm on metoprolol and hydrochlorothiazide for blood pressure. And a baby aspirin.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: I'd like to check orthostatic vitals and get an EKG.

full_text_reference: Hello, Ms. Smith. Tell me what happened. I passed out this morning. I was standing in the kitchen and the next thing I knew I was on the floor. Any warning before you passed out? I felt lightheaded, a bit sweaty, and my vision went gray around the edges. How long were you out? My husband said maybe ten seconds. I came to quickly. Any chest pain, palpitations, or shortness of breath before it happened? No chest pain. My heart felt like it was racing for a second, but it was quick. Did you bite your tongue or lose bladder control? No, nothing like that. You said your heart was racing — was that before or after you felt lightheaded? Actually, thinking about it, the racing came first. Then the lightheaded feeling, then I went down. Okay, that's important. Any similar episodes before? I've felt dizzy when I stand up quickly, but I've never passed out before. Any recent illness, fluid loss, or changes in medication? I had a stomach bug two days ago, some diarrhea. I haven't been drinking much water. What medications do you take? I'm on metoprolol and hydrochlorothiazide for blood pressure. And a baby aspirin. I'd like to check orthostatic vitals and get an EKG.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Hi, I'm Doctor Patel. What's going on with Emma today?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: She's had a fever for five days now. It keeps going up and down. The highest was a hundred and three last night.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Any other symptoms along with the fever?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: She has this rash. It started on her trunk and now it's spread to her arms too. And her lips look really red and cracked.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: When did the rash first show up?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: Thursday, I think. Actually wait, no, it was Friday — Thursday she just had the fever, the rash showed up Friday morning.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Okay, Friday then. How old is Emma now, and what does she weigh?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: She's fourteen months, and she was about ten kilos at her last check.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Any trouble with feeding, sleep, or her usual behavior?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: She's been really cranky, won't eat much, and mostly just wants to sleep.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: What have you been giving her for the fever?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: Tylenol. The infant one, a hundred and sixty milligrams per five milliliters. I've been giving her about three milliliters every six hours.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Let me come back to that dose in a minute — at ten kilos she's probably underdosed. Is she up to date on her vaccinations?

turns.speaker: PATIENT
turns.text: Yes, everything except the MMR. That one's scheduled for next month.

turns.speaker: DOCTOR
turns.text: Let me take a look at her mouth. I can see those strawberry tongue bumps and the cracked lips you described. With five days of fever, rash on her trunk and extremities, and these oral changes, I want to work her up for Kawasaki disease today.

Let me take a look at her mouth. I can see those strawberry tongue bumps and the cracked lips you described.
